text:
“More than one thing,” said Lord Ebbing, and motioned for the two men to follow. “Here on both sides—do you see these marks? At first I thought grapnels, but beside the deeper gouges there are smaller ones, as if from clawed hands. See? And this here is something else altogether, some kind of tool. If only I could see the place where the ship was holed.”

“There are marks like these up on the deck too. Do you think our sea monster—”

“Monsters, Mr. André, monsters. It’s a shoal. A whole shoal of monsters, perhaps human-sized, judging by the marks.”

Will laughed. “Tell me I’m hearing this wrong, and you’re not saying they were Sirens?”

Caleb didn’t laugh. He had come to the same conclusion as Lord Ebbing, however impossible it seemed. Or like something out of a nursery tale.

Seeing their grim faces, Will’s smile faded.

“There is a great deal we don’t know about the ocean, Mr. Nealy. Most children’s tales have roots in reality. I don’t need to tell you our next step, Mr. André.”

Caleb sighed. More nights in the library awaited him, pages of notes. And besides zoology, meteorology, and geology, it seemed he would have to haunt the children’s shelves and the mythology stacks as well.
